video_title: “We Will Have a Crash”: Why AI Brings Out the Fear in People
gist: Visser argues the AI trade is entering a new two-sided regime where supply-chain bottlenecks (memory, optical, gas turbines, semi test equipment, power) are the real second-half risk, not a 'bubble.' He stays long the 'physical-world upgrade' thematic basket (chemicals, industrials, power, optical) over the hyperscalers, dismisses crash-callers as psychologically motivated, and lays out a technical (20/50/200-day MA) discipline for riding bubbles. He flags oil/energy as the key macro swing factor, sees pharma (esp. Eli Lilly) as the next AI-native winner, and stays structurally bullish Bitcoin/tokenization despite calling crypto a current bear market.

themes.theme: New AI regime: breadth breakdown & bottleneck risk

insights.claim: Visser says the S&P is up ~5% on the month but only 3 of 11 sectors are higher, with gains concentrated almost entirely in AI semis, hardware and power — a narrowing-breadth signal he reads as the AI trade entering a two-sided regime rather than continued one-way melt-up.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Watch sector breadth and the AI-vs-rest spread; a regime where 'crappy beta' outperforms high-earnings AI names is a top-building, not crash, signal to trim into.
insights.quote: only three of them were up

insights.claim: Visser warns AI supply-chain bottlenecks (memory, optical, copper, silver, gas turbines, NAND, semiconductor test equipment) create real production risk for H2 2026 earnings that the market is not pricing, citing Fuji Kura's sharp post-earnings drop and Modine flagging supply constraints despite strong numbers.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: The H2 risk is delivery/buildout delay, not demand; monitor optical/memory names' guidance for bottleneck language as the catalyst for the next AI-trade drawdown.
insights.quote: I think the market is not building in the production risk for the second half of the year, which I think is going to be greater than what people think.

insights.claim: Visser cites Morgan Stanley data that semis, hardware and power have accounted for 97% of long/short fund long returns year-to-date, with the remaining 75% of long books up less than 1% — evidence of extreme crowding in the AI complex.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Returns this concentrated mean any bottleneck-driven stall in AI hardware hits the entire hedge-fund complex at once — elevated correlated drawdown risk.
insights.quote: semis hardware and power have accounted for 97% of long short fund long returns year to date with the remaining 75% of long portfolio up less than 1% year-to date

insights.claim: Visser flags Korea retail-mania signals — record stock-trading accounts, record margin, teenagers and family gatherings discussing surging shares — as classic peak behavior that has shifted the risk/reward against AI/memory longs even if the trend continues.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Frothy retail participation in Korea is a sentiment caution flag for the global AI/memory trade; treat it as a reason to manage size, not to short outright.
insights.quote: margin is at the highest level record ever. Those types of sayings are absolutely consistent with peaks in markets

insights.claim: Visser's AI-specific 'turbulence model' fired seven turbulent signals in May — the most of any month — while his broad-market turbulence model still reads completely healthy, signaling stress is concentrated inside the AI complex rather than market-wide.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Localized AI turbulence with a calm broad market argues for trimming AI beta specifically rather than de-risking the whole portfolio.
insights.quote: I had seven turbulent signals in the month of May. We hadn't had any month that big.

insights.claim: Visser's core framework: never try to pick a bubble top — instead wait for the 20-day MA to cross below the 50-day after all-time highs and exit/stay-out once price breaks a down-turning 200-day MA, which historically (1929, 1987, 2000, 2008) lets you avoid the bulk of a crash while giving up only a small portion of gains.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A rules-based MA-cross exit lets investors stay long parabolic AI names while capping downside — actionable risk management vs. binary bubble bets.
insights.quote: I'm not going to bail out of anything or worry about a bubble until the 20-day moving average crosses the 50-day moving average.

insights.claim: Visser argues crashes don't wipe you out in a day — bubbles build tops over time, so the danger is being bearish and out the entire way up; the asymmetry of bubble-calling (look brilliant if it crashes, claim 'bubbles go further' if it doesn't) makes it a socially low-risk but financially costly stance.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Discount pundit crash calls; the opportunity cost of sitting out a parabolic AI advance is larger than the avoided drawdown if you use MA stops.
insights.quote: Bubble calling is asymmetrical. If prices crash, they look brilliant. If prices keep rising, they can say bubbles always go further than what people expect.

insights.claim: Visser dismisses the Buffett indicator and GDP-based valuation metrics as obsolete because GDP was built for the industrial revolution and cannot measure intangibles or AI output, arguing expanding profit margins and rising earnings justify current prices.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: If you believe traditional valuation gauges are broken, market-cap/GDP 'bubble' warnings lose force — lean on price/earnings momentum instead.
insights.quote: It does not handle intangibles. It does not handle what is going on in AI. It is a stupid thing to pay attention to as the only metric.

insights.claim: Visser notes markets have already had three 20%+ corrections (two of 30%+) in the past three years with confidence notably low, arguing this is the opposite of classic bubble conditions where confidence is euphoric.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Recent repeated corrections plus low confidence undercut the 'unsustainable euphoria' bubble narrative; expect another ~20% correction but not a structural top.
insights.quote: We've had three 20 plus% corrections. Two of them 30% or higher over the course of the last three years.

insights.claim: Visser sees no systemic leverage to fuel a crash — credit spreads are benign and the main IG issuance is from cash-flush AI hyperscalers whose equity dwarfs their debt, so he is unworried about an AI-driven credit event near-term.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Absent leverage and widening spreads, a 1929/2008-style cascade is unlikely; the risk is an equity correction, not a credit blowup — for now.
insights.quote: right now they're just flush with cash and more importantly their equity is worth far more than the debt that they have

insights.claim: Visser's central trade is the 'benchmark arbitrage': underweight the hyperscalers/spenders and overweight the suppliers they pay — chemicals, industrials, power, optical and select semis — because capex-heavy spenders with rising receivables and debt are vulnerable to a buildout slowdown while the receivers benefit.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Long AI-supplier basket / short hyperscalers is positioned as both an outperformance trade and a hedge against a buildout stall.
insights.quote: I don't see the spenders outperforming the receivers in any way, shape, or form except for consolidations and rotations.

insights.claim: Visser cites Jensen Huang's $90 trillion AI buildout figure over the next 10-15 years — nearly 85% of today's global economy — spread across energy, chips and infrastructure, as the structural demand backstop for his physical-upgrade thesis.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A multi-decade $90T buildout frames AI capex as secular, supporting 'first or second inning' positioning in suppliers despite near-term bottlenecks.
insights.quote: It's a $90 trillion buildout. $90 trillion, which is almost 85% of the global economy today.

insights.claim: Visser argues chemicals are replacing oil as the key intensity-versus-GDP input over the next five years and are critical to AI infrastructure, making them a core overweight in his thematic portfolio.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Positions chemicals as a non-obvious AI-infrastructure play distinct from semis — a way to get AI exposure outside crowded chip names.
insights.quote: chemicals, in my opinion, are replacing oil in terms of intensity versus GDP over the next 5 years

insights.claim: Visser holds Dell as validation of the physical-upgrade thesis after it raised FY2027 EPS guidance to $18 from $12, lifted revenue guidance to $165-195B from $138-142B, beat by $8B and grew 88% y/y — arguing the parabolic chart is justified by fundamentals, not a bubble.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Dell's guidance raise is concrete proof the AI hardware buildout is real-revenue driven; cited as a counter to chart-based bubble calls.
insights.quote: Dell raised its fullear fiscal 2027 earnings guidance to $18 per share from 12.

insights.claim: Visser cites Cisco's G2 Patel that AI raised projected enterprise WAN/network traffic growth from ~2.5x to ~9x over the next decade — a 4x repricing of the entire networking opportunity — which he calls the single statement explaining why optical names like Corning have gone parabolic.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A 4x demand repricing of network traffic is the fundamental engine behind optical/fiber names — supports staying long optical despite Fuji Kura's bottleneck wobble.
insights.quote: without AI was uh agentic AI was projected to grow about two and a half times over the next decade but now it's jumped to nine times

insights.claim: Visser notes Goldman Sachs expects only 50-60% of data-center capacity scheduled for the next one-to-two years to actually come online, reinforcing the bottleneck/shortage thesis driving supplier earnings.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Sub-60% capacity delivery means chronic shortage pricing power for suppliers but execution risk for hyperscaler ROI timelines.
insights.quote: Only about 50 to 60% of data center capacity scheduled for the next one to two years is expected to come online.

insights.claim: Visser flags a Goldman chart showing trailing-12-month capex dollar growth has hit 60% — the highest since the dot-com bubble — while growth-stock cash levels sit near 25-year lows, a combination he views as buildout intensity rather than a red flag.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Record capex growth plus low cash buffers raises fragility if revenue lags spend — the key thing to monitor for the buildout thesis to break.
insights.quote: trailing 12-month capital spending dollar growth has reached 60% the highest reading since right before the do or right during the dotcom bubble

insights.claim: Visser exited Micron around an average of ~$600-650 (after riding it from ~$100) and rotated into silver and Bitcoin, arguing that even though Micron rose another ~50% the risk/reward shifted because all three memory makers hit $1T market caps and the move (~1,000% in 15 months) is unlikely to repeat.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A named CIO-style rotation out of crowded memory into silver/Bitcoin signals where he sees better forward risk/reward despite near-term underperformance.
insights.quote: I do believe from here the riskreward has changed significantly.

insights.claim: Visser is wary that memory's cyclicality may not be permanently 'fixed' — algorithmic efficiency gains, Chinese competition, or simply more supply could reassert the cycle even as bulls assume this time is different.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Bet against the 'memory is now secular' consensus; watch for algorithmic/Chinese supply catalysts that could re-introduce the cycle and cap memory multiples.
insights.quote: you got to bet that the memory situation is not going to be cyclical this time and that we're not going to be able to make more of it. I'm a little bit more wary about that situation.

themes.theme: Macro: oil, inflation & power as the swing risk

insights.claim: Visser names rising oil as the most likely trigger for a 20-30% correction: he'd buy oil stocks into weakness as a hedge, arguing if oil pushes higher from here the broad market falls, and oil names stay cheap and under-owned around $80-85.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Accumulate energy on dips as a cheap correction hedge; oil breaking higher is the macro tripwire to watch for the next equity drawdown.
insights.quote: if oil goes higher, then the market's going to go down. I would be putting on oil into weakness here.

insights.claim: Visser cites Exxon's and Chevron's super-majors warning that oil could hit $160 within weeks as global inventories approach record lows, with Chevron's Mike Wirth saying we're 'weeks away from oil shortages' — a tail risk amplified if Iran disrupts Strait of Hormuz flows to influence the midterms.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A geopolitically-driven oil spike to $160 would simultaneously stoke inflation and pressure equities — the cleanest macro hedge case in the episode.
insights.quote: We had Exxon and Chevron both out this week saying oil prices could hit 160 within weeks.

insights.claim: Visser sees inflation re-accelerating as a genuine correction risk now that temporary buffers (tariff front-running, etc.) have been exhausted, with commodity prices rising and a new Fed chair entering — a setup that could force the Fed's hand.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Inflation reasserting after buffers run out is the macro fundamental that could justify a 20-30% pullback — watch CPI and commodity prints.
insights.quote: We've gone through the temporary buffers

insights.claim: Visser warns grid/power blackouts are a real risk — summer peak load passed 135 GW with reserves at only 4% — and an East Coast blackout would be bearish for stocks because it could force a pullback in AI usage.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Grid fragility is an under-appreciated AI-trade risk; a blackout headline could be the catalyst that forces AI-demand and equity de-rating.
insights.quote: Summer heat drove peak load past 135 gigawatts with reserves at 4%.

insights.claim: Visser maintains there are no recessions or business cycles in the modern economy because the world no longer depends on credit and GDP no longer measures real activity — a view he says he seeded with Raoul Pal.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: If business cycles are obsolete, traditional recession-hedging and cyclical timing frameworks misfire — stay structurally long secular AI growth.
insights.quote: I think I was the first person to say there's no such thing as recessions

insights.claim: Visser endorses Raoul Pal/Julien Bittel's framing that the economy is shifting from capital-and-labor dependency to compute-and-energy dependency — the structural reason he says business cycles no longer exist.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A compute/energy-driven economy reorients where alpha lives — toward compute, energy and infrastructure rather than traditional cyclicals.
insights.quote: We're leaving a world that's been dependent on capital and labor and we're moving into a world that's entirely dependent on comput and energy.

insights.claim: Visser endorses Torsten Slok's reframing of asset allocation as 'the new 60/40 is AI vs non-AI,' agreeing that single-factor over-exposure is the real risk and that most portfolios are dangerously underweight AI.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Reframe diversification around AI exposure; the index Visser built with Morgan Stanley exists to close advisors' AI underweight gap.
insights.quote: The new 6040 is AI verse non AI.

insights.claim: Visser argues AI will disrupt every traditional wealth-management asset class over the next five years — he sees VC, private credit, private equity and real estate all failing to come back — pushing advisors toward AI infrastructure, pharma and crypto for alpha.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A blunt call that legacy private-asset allocations underperform structurally — reposition toward AI infra, pharma and crypto/tokenization.
insights.quote: I don't see VC coming back. I don't see PC coming back. I don't see PE coming back. I don't see real estate coming back.

insights.claim: Visser highlights Mark Rowan's point that the AI buildout breaks the VC model because the VC ecosystem was never capital-intensive, whereas today's fast-growing AI labs need enormous capital and must tap debt markets and go public.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Capital-intensive AI shifts financing from VC to debt/public markets — bullish for credit issuance and IPO pipelines tied to AI infrastructure.
insights.quote: you have an entire VC ecosystem that has never been capital intensive

insights.claim: Visser relays Dan Shipper's (Lenny's Podcast) view that agents do not eliminate SaaS but change its surface — tools like Codex or 'Corowick' become the new operating layer where users work inside an AI environment that sees the browser instead of opening 10 separate SaaS apps.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: The agentic-AI-kills-SaaS short thesis is overstated; SaaS persists in a reconfigured form — nuance for software-sector positioning.
insights.quote: agents do not eliminate SAS

insights.claim: Visser cautions that despite heavy spend on Claude/AI licenses, companies are questioning whether soaring AI spending is delivering returns, and adoption-driven productivity will arrive in 'fits and starts' rather than smoothly — a reason to watch S&P margins ex-hardware.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Lagging AI ROI means software/enterprise margin gains disappoint near-term; strip out hardware to see the true (weaker) margin picture.
insights.quote: companies are starting to question whether soaring AI spending is delivering returns

insights.claim: Visser notes DeepSeek made its 75% price discount permanent and that cheap, improving Chinese models are a structural pricing-power threat to hyperscalers even if Western firms hesitate to adopt them.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Chinese model price deflation pressures hyperscaler AI economics — a margin headwind reinforcing the underweight-spenders stance.
insights.quote: Deepseek makes it 75% discount permanent.

insights.claim: Visser cites Anthropic raising $65B this week at a ~$965B valuation with Brad Gerstner and Sequoia involved, arguing sophisticated late-stage investors at that level signal the AI revenue trajectory is real, not retail-driven.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A ~$965B Anthropic round backed by top funds is offered as evidence the AI buildout has institutional conviction — supports the secular thesis.
insights.quote: Anthropic also raised 65 billion this week at a 965 billion.

themes.theme: Pharma as the next AI-native winner

insights.claim: Visser argues AI is rebuilding drug discovery around foundational models and revaluing pharma's 'buried' failed-trial data and IP — letting companies take stalled-at-the-20-yard-line drugs to the goal line — making pharma a distinct human-software beneficiary rather than enterprise-productivity software.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: AI re-rates pharma's dormant IP; positions drug discovery as an under-owned AI vertical with M&A and revaluation upside.
insights.quote: Failed data is still a great idea that now is useful.

insights.claim: Visser holds Eli Lilly as the only pharma name in his thematic portfolio, citing 55% y/y revenue growth, a PEG below 1, new all-time highs, ~2x the performance of any Mag Seven name since end-2017, plus its 1,000-Blackwell 'Lily Pod' supercomputer and Nvidia partnership.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Eli Lilly is framed as an accelerating AI-native pharma compounder at a sub-1 PEG — a specific long combining GLP-1 growth and AI optionality.
insights.quote: They have a PEG ratio below one.

insights.claim: Visser highlights Eli Lilly's aggressive AI/gene-editing M&A — the $22M Engaged deal, a Bezos-backed Proffluent gene-editing partnership, ramped Insilico ties, a $1B Nvidia drug-discovery lab, and the Verve Therapeutics acquisition yielding early data on a one-time gene-editing cholesterol therapy.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Lilly's gene-editing dealmaking targets the world's #1 killer (heart disease) with one-time cures — a concrete pipeline catalyst to track.
insights.quote: They acquired Verve Therapeutics to advance one-time treatments for people with cardiovascular risk.

insights.claim: Visser frames AI-driven drug discovery as a societal necessity because U.S. health spending is heading to ~20% of nominal GDP (~$6 trillion/year) and entitlement costs will exceed revenues unless AI makes people healthier — a structural tailwind for pharma AI adopters.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A ~$6T health market plus fiscal pressure forces AI adoption in pharma — sizes the addressable opportunity for AI-native drug companies.
insights.quote: it's very quickly becoming 20% of nominal GDP in terms of health spending which means it is about $6 trillion a year

insights.claim: Visser cites Gavin Baker (All-In) that AI's most powerful positive story is ordinary motivated people using LLMs to attack disease discovery directly — turning individuals into research teams and expanding (not eliminating) biotech, especially for rare diseases.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: AI broadens biotech participation rather than commoditizing it — bullish breadth signal for the drug-discovery vertical.
insights.quote: AI turns motivated humans into research teams.

themes.theme: Crypto bear market but structural conviction

insights.claim: Visser declares Bitcoin and crypto are in a clear bear market — trading below a down-turning 200-day MA that rejects every rally, with nearly four years of unchanged price at the lows — and advises waiting to buy until price reclaims the 200-day rather than catching the falling knife.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Discipline over conviction: don't add to Bitcoin until it reclaims the 200-day; that reclaim is the trigger to deploy aggressively.
insights.quote: That is the absolute definition of a bare market.

insights.claim: Visser remains structurally bullish, calling Bitcoin 'the S&P 500 of the future of the capital system' and expecting it materially higher next year, driven by parabolic growth in stablecoins and tokenization that he says is just beginning.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Frames Bitcoin as the benchmark asset of a tokenized future — a long-horizon accumulation thesis once the technical signal turns.
insights.quote: Bitcoin is basically the S&P 500 of the future of the capital system.

insights.claim: Visser flags bearish flow/sentiment in crypto — IBIT gave up all recent gains, Bitcoin ETFs posted the second-worst month ever for net flows, Ethereum ETH shares outstanding keep falling, and Polymarket odds on the Clarity Act passing fell from 75% back to 50% — explaining current non-participation.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Record-bad ETF outflows plus stalled crypto legislation are concrete reasons crypto lags now — watch flows and Clarity Act odds for a turn.
insights.quote: we posted the second worst month ever in terms of inflows versus outflows in the ETF

insights.claim: Visser reads bearish macro commentators (Odd Lots-style) as a contrarian crypto-positive signal, noting their crypto 'capitulation' tone and willingness to call a possible low even as everything they cited — social instability, inflation, market manipulation — he interprets as pro-crypto.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Bearish-pundit capitulation is a soft contrarian buy signal for crypto — monitor sentiment extremes alongside the 200-day technical trigger.
insights.quote: it felt more like a capitulation type thing

insights.claim: Visser has built a tokenized 40-name (34 tokens + 6 public stocks) thematic crypto basket mirroring his AI approach and will launch dedicated crypto YouTube content this year, with the agentic-crypto sleeve up ~40% YTD.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Signals where Visser sees intra-crypto alpha (agentic/tokenization sleeves) and an upcoming product/content push around tokenization.
insights.quote: I've created a tokenized 34 tokens, six public stocks with thematic breakdowns
